What is a bay plan?

A bay plan is a numbering system that gives a cross-sectional view of how containers are arranged on a vessel, both above and below the deck. During cargo operations it is often called a discharging and loading plan, and it is used to determine the correct stowage position of each container. When comparing the plan with the ship, you read it looking forward towards the bow.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 1: Bay Plan Definition (Discharging/Loading Plan) (slide p.1-3)

How is a six-digit stow position structured?

A stow position has 6 digits: the first two indicate the Bay, the middle two indicate the Row, and the last two indicate the Tier (Bay-Row-Tier). For example, 110910 = Bay 11, Row 09, Tier 10.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 2: Six-Digit Stow Position Format (Bay-Row-Tier) (slide p.4-4)

How are bays numbered along the ship?

Bays are numbered from bay 01 at the fore and increase towards the stern, continuing up to a maximum (for example bay 54) depending on the size of the vessel.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 3: Bay Numbering & Range (slide p.5-5)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
How do bay numbers distinguish 20' and 40' containers?

20-foot containers occupy odd-numbered bays, while 40-foot containers are assigned even-numbered bays. When a pair of 20-footer bays is used as a 40-footer bay (fixed or convertible), the 40-footer bay takes the even number lying between the two odd 20-footer bay numbers fore and aft.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 4: 20' Odd Bays vs 40' Even Bays (slide p.6-15)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
How are rows numbered across the ship's width?

Row indicates the container's position across the width (breadth) of the vessel, counted from the centre line outward. A container on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side (01, 03, 05, 07...) and even-numbered on the port side (02, 04, 06, 08...). This prevents confusion between port and starboard containers.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 5: Row Numbering (Centerline 00, Starboard Odd / Port Even) (slide p.8-22)

How are tiers numbered on deck versus in the hold?

Tier is the vertical level of a container in its stack, counted from the bottom upward. On deck, tier numbering normally starts at 82, 84, 86 and upward. Under deck (in the hold), tiers are numbered 02, 04, 06 and so on, starting from the bottom. Tier 02 represents a standard-height container at the lowest location of a bay; ordinary-height containers use only even numbers.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 6: Tier Numbering (Deck 82+, Hold 02+ from Bottom) (slide p.12-23)

Where can bay and row numbers be physically found on a ship?

Bay numbers are welded and painted on the ship's coamings or hatch covers. Row numbers are likewise welded and painted on the hatch covers and inside the holds.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 7: Bay/Row Markings on the Ship (Coamings, Hatch Covers, Holds) (slide p.8-10)

What three factors identify a cell position in a stowage plan?

Cell positions are identified by three factors: Bay - counted from fore to aft; Row - counted from the ship's centre line to portside or starboard side; and Tier - counted in the vertical direction from bottom upward.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 8: Three-Factor Cell Identification (Fore-Aft / Centerline / Vertical) (slide p.14-14)

What common abbreviations appear on a bay plan?

Common bay plan abbreviations include Reefer (refrigerated), OOG/Over-size (out of gauge), Empty, OT (Open Top), HC (High-Cube), IMDG (dangerous goods), and Restow.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 9: Bay Plan Abbreviations (Reefer, OOG, Empty, OT, HC, IMDG, Restow) (slide p.17-17)

Decode the stow position with Bay 14, Tier 84 and Tier 02 - what do they mean?

Bay 14 is an even bay, indicating a 40-foot (fore-aft) position. Tier 84 is an on-deck tier (deck numbering starts around 82/84), here the lowest deck tier. Tier 02 is the bottom-most tier located inside the hold (under deck). Together with the row, these give the container's unique Bay-Row-Tier coordinate.

Why does the Bay-Row-Tier system exist?

Bay-Row-Tier exists to manage thousands of containers onboard by giving every slot a unique coordinate. It lets crews know exact container storage, understand load and discharge plans, and coordinate with crane operators, officers, and stevedores.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 11: Why Bay-Row-Tier Exists (Unique Coordinate) (slide p.20-20)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is a bay, and how does a 40' container relate to bay spaces?

A bay is a section of the ship cut across its width (a fore-aft position). Bays are odd-numbered (01, 03, 05...) for 20-foot positions, and a single 40-foot container occupies two adjacent 20-foot bay spaces.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 12: Bay as Fore-Aft Section; 40' Occupies Two 20' Spaces (slide p.21-21)

Why must deck cadets understand the Bay-Row-Tier system?

Mastering Bay-Row-Tier is crucial for safe, accurate, efficient operations: cargo watches (verify placement and container numbers, prevent shifting), lashing operations (identify correct stacks to secure), reefer checks (locate reefers, read/record temperatures), and hatch operations (know which bays/holds are affected by cover removal).

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 14: Operational Importance for Deck Cadets (slide p.24-24)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
How does a Chief Officer use the Bay-Row-Tier system?

A Chief Officer relies on Bay-Row-Tier for critical decisions: cross-referencing container data, designating special container areas, and identifying and managing hazardous material (IMDG) locations. Mastering it early avoids confusion and prevents delays, re-shifting, penalties, and safety risks.

[출처] Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 15: How Officers Use Bay-Row-Tier (Chief Officer Decisions) (slide p.25-25)

Provide a grounded explanation of '20' Odd Bays vs 40' Even Bays' for reading a bay plan.

Per Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), 20' Odd Bays vs 40' Even Bays: A 20-foot container will occupy odd-numbered bays, whereas 40-foot containers are numbered with even-numbered bays. Bay numbers are indicated by odd numbers from fore to aft; odd-numbered bays show 20-foot container bays. When a pair of 20-footer bays is used as a 40-footer bay (either fixed or convertible), this 40-footer bay is represented by an even number that lies between the odd numbers representing the 20-footer bays fore and aft.

The row shows the position of the container across the width of the vessel. A container placed on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side like 01, 03, 05, 07; rows are even-numbered on the port side like 02, 04, 06, 08. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 fe

Provide a grounded explanation of 'Tier Numbering (Deck 82+, Hold 02+ from Bottom)' for reading a bay plan.

Per Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Tier Numbering (Deck 82+, Hold 02+ from Bottom): If the container is placed on deck, tier numbering normally starts from 82, 84, 86 and upward. Containers stowed under the deck are numbered 02, 04, 06 and so on, starting from the bottom. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 feet 6 inches for 20-footers, 8 feet 6 inches for 40-footers) at the lowest location of a bay. For ordinary-height containers only even numbers are used.

The first deck tier often starts at 82 or 84. Even numbers ensure clear vertical identification. Cadets must know hold vs deck tiers and ship-specific numbering. Putting it all together: decode a stow position such as Bay 14 (fore-aft), Tier 84 (on deck, lowest tier) or Tier 02 (bottom-most tier inside the hold).

Reefer checks - locate reefers, read temperatures, and record data. Hatch operations - know which bays/holds are affected by cover removal. Mastering Bay-Row-Tier ensures speed and accuracy in all tasks.
